  • Automated Amazon affiliate Pinterest engine with n8n
  • How compound interest works
Claude finished the response

You said: "I am a Developer Student building an automated Amazon Affiliate engine.

"I am a Developer Student building an automated Amazon Affiliate engine. I need you to help me build an n8n workflow (running in Docker) to scale my Pinterest operations. The Stack: * Source: Google Sheets (Columns: URL, Category, Affiliate_Link). * Logic: n8n. * AI Node: Gemini/Claude API for writing content. * Images: Python script using PIL (Pillow) to overlay text on product images. * Target: Pinterest Business API. The Task: 1. Write a Python script I can run inside an n8n 'Execute Command' node that takes an Amazon URL and returns the Product Name and Main Image URL using BeautifulSoup or Playwright. 2. Create the JSON structure for an n8n workflow that: * Triggers every 4 hours. * Reads a 'Pending' row from Google Sheets. * Calls an LLM to generate a Pinterest Title (Max 100 chars) and Description (Max 500 chars) specifically for 'Engineering Students' living in hostels. * Uses the Python script to generate a 2:3 vertical Pinterest image. * Uploads the image to Pinterest with the 'Affiliate_Link' as the destination. STRICT REQUIREMENT: No sugarcoating. Use professional developer terminology. Ensure the code is optimized for low-resource Docker environments."

21 Mar

Claude responded: This is a pure developer/architecture task — no frontend skill needed.

Reading the skill files relevant to this task

This is a pure developer/architecture task — no frontend skill needed. Let me build this out properly.

Here's the complete, production-ready implementation split into two deliverables.

Part 1 — Python Scraper (scrape_product.py)

This runs inside n8n's Execute Command node. Uses playwright with --no-sandbox flags for Docker compatibility. Falls back to requests + BeautifulSoup for lighter environments.

python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
scrape_product.py
Usage: python3 scrape_product.py "<amazon_url>"
Output: JSON to stdout — { "product_name": str, "image_url": str, "error": str|null }

Strategy:
  1. Try requests + BeautifulSoup (zero-browser, lowest RAM).
  2. If blocked (bot detection), fall back to Playwright Chromium (headless).

Docker requirements:
  pip install requests beautifulsoup4 lxml playwright
  playwright install chromium --with-deps
"""

import sys
import json
import re

def scrape_with_requests(url: str) -> dict:
    import requests
    from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

    HEADERS = {
        "User-Agent": (
            "Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) "
            "AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) "
            "Chrome/124.0.0.0 Safari/537.36"
        ),
        "Accept-Language": "en-US,en;q=0.9",
        "Accept-Encoding": "gzip, deflate, br",
        "Accept": "text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,*/*;q=0.8",
        "DNT": "1",
    }

    resp = requests.get(url, headers=HEADERS, timeout=15)
    resp.raise_for_status()

    if "api-services-support@amazon.com" in resp.text:
        raise ValueError("Bot-detected by Amazon. Switching to Playwright.")

    soup = BeautifulSoup(resp.text, "lxml")

    # Product title — multiple selector fallbacks
    name = None
    for sel in ["#productTitle", "#title", "h1.a-size-large"]:
        tag = soup.select_one(sel)
        if tag:
            name = tag.get_text(strip=True)
            break

    # Main image — Amazon stores it in a JS blob; parse raw HTML
    image_url = None
    img_tag = soup.select_one("#landingImage, #imgBlkFront, #main-image")
    if img_tag:
        image_url = img_tag.get("data-old-hires") or img_tag.get("src")

    # Fallback: extract from inline JSON (colorImages / imageGalleryData)
    if not image_url:
        match = re.search(r'"hiRes":"(https://m\.media-amazon\.com/images/[^"]+)"', resp.text)
        if match:
            image_url = match.group(1)

    if not name or not image_url:
        raise ValueError(f"Parsed name={name!r}, image={image_url!r}. Incomplete.")

    return {"product_name": name, "image_url": image_url, "error": None}


def scrape_with_playwright(url: str) -> dict:
    from playwright.sync_api import sync_playwright

    with sync_playwright() as p:
        browser = p.chromium.launch(
            headless=True,
            args=[
                "--no-sandbox",
                "--disable-setuid-sandbox",
                "--disable-dev-shm-usage",   # critical for Docker /dev/shm limits
                "--disable-gpu",
                "--single-process",
            ],
        )
        page = browser.new_page(
            user_agent=(
                "Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) "
                "AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) "
                "Chrome/124.0.0.0 Safari/537.36"
            )
        )
        page.goto(url, wait_until="domcontentloaded", timeout=30000)

        name = page.locator("#productTitle").first.inner_text().strip()

        # Trigger image load
        image_url = page.evaluate("""() => {
            const img = document.querySelector('#landingImage, #imgBlkFront');
            return img ? (img.getAttribute('data-old-hires') || img.src) : null;
        }""")

        browser.close()

    if not name or not image_url:
        raise ValueError("Playwright extraction incomplete.")

    return {"product_name": name, "image_url": image_url, "error": None}


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) < 2:
        print(json.dumps({"error": "No URL provided.", "product_name": None, "image_url": None}))
        sys.exit(1)

    target_url = sys.argv[1]
    result = {}

    try:
        result = scrape_with_requests(target_url)
    except Exception as e1:
        try:
            result = scrape_with_playwright(target_url)
        except Exception as e2:
            result = {
                "product_name": None,
                "image_url": None,
                "error": f"requests: {e1} | playwright: {e2}",
            }

    print(json.dumps(result))

Part 2 — PIL Image Generator (generate_pin_image.py)

Produces a 1000×1500px (2:3) Pinterest-optimized image. Runs in the same Execute Command node.

python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
generate_pin_image.py
Usage: python3 generate_pin_image.py "<image_url>" "<title_text>" "<output_path>"
Output: Saves JPEG to output_path, prints path to stdout.

Dependencies: pip install pillow requests
"""

import sys
import os
import json
import requests
from io import BytesIO
from PIL import Image, ImageDraw, ImageFont, ImageFilter

PIN_W, PIN_H = 1000, 1500
FONT_PATH = "/usr/share/fonts/truetype/dejavu/DejaVuSans-Bold.ttf"  # pre-installed in most Linux Docker images
FALLBACK_FONT_SIZE = 48
GRADIENT_STRENGTH = 200  # 0-255 alpha at darkest


def download_image(url: str) -> Image.Image:
    resp = requests.get(url, timeout=15)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    img = Image.open(BytesIO(resp.content)).convert("RGB")
    return img


def fit_and_crop(img: Image.Image, target_w: int, target_h: int) -> Image.Image:
    """Scale-to-fill then center-crop. Avoids distortion."""
    src_w, src_h = img.size
    scale = max(target_w / src_w, target_h / src_h)
    new_w = int(src_w * scale)
    new_h = int(src_h * scale)
    img = img.resize((new_w, new_h), Image.LANCZOS)
    left = (new_w - target_w) // 2
    top = (new_h - target_h) // 2
    return img.crop((left, top, left + target_w, top + target_h))


def add_gradient_overlay(img: Image.Image) -> Image.Image:
    """Bottom-up dark gradient for text legibility."""
    gradient = Image.new("L", (PIN_W, PIN_H), 0)
    draw = ImageDraw.Draw(gradient)
    for y in range(PIN_H):
        # Linear ramp: top=transparent, bottom=GRADIENT_STRENGTH
        alpha = int((y / PIN_H) ** 2 * GRADIENT_STRENGTH)
        draw.line([(0, y), (PIN_W, y)], fill=alpha)

    black_layer = Image.new("RGB", (PIN_W, PIN_H), (0, 0, 0))
    img = Image.composite(black_layer, img, gradient)
    return img


def wrap_text(text: str, font: ImageFont.FreeTypeFont, max_width: int) -> list[str]:
    words = text.split()
    lines, current = [], ""
    for word in words:
        test = f"{current} {word}".strip()
        bbox = font.getbbox(test)
        if bbox[2] <= max_width:
            current = test
        else:
            if current:
                lines.append(current)
            current = word
    if current:
        lines.append(current)
    return lines


def render_text(img: Image.Image, title: str) -> Image.Image:
    draw = ImageDraw.Draw(img)

    try:
        font = ImageFont.truetype(FONT_PATH, FALLBACK_FONT_SIZE)
    except IOError:
        font = ImageFont.load_default()

    margin = 60
    max_text_width = PIN_W - (margin * 2)
    lines = wrap_text(title, font, max_text_width)

    line_height = FALLBACK_FONT_SIZE + 12
    total_text_height = len(lines) * line_height
    y_start = PIN_H - total_text_height - 80  # 80px from bottom

    for line in lines:
        bbox = font.getbbox(line)
        x = (PIN_W - (bbox[2] - bbox[0])) // 2  # center-aligned
        # Drop shadow for contrast
        draw.text((x + 2, y_start + 2), line, font=font, fill=(0, 0, 0, 180))
        draw.text((x, y_start), line, font=font, fill=(255, 255, 255, 255))
        y_start += line_height

    return img


def add_brand_badge(img: Image.Image, label: str = "🎓 Hostel Engineer Picks") -> Image.Image:
    draw = ImageDraw.Draw(img)
    try:
        font = ImageFont.truetype(FONT_PATH, 28)
    except IOError:
        font = ImageFont.load_default()

    pad_x, pad_y = 20, 10
    bbox = font.getbbox(label)
    rect_x1, rect_y1 = 40, 40
    rect_x2 = rect_x1 + bbox[2] + pad_x * 2
    rect_y2 = rect_y1 + bbox[3] + pad_y * 2

    draw.rounded_rectangle([rect_x1, rect_y1, rect_x2, rect_y2], radius=8, fill=(255, 90, 0))
    draw.text((rect_x1 + pad_x, rect_y1 + pad_y), label, font=font, fill=(255, 255, 255))
    return img


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) < 4:
        print(json.dumps({"error": "Usage: script.py <image_url> <title> <output_path>"}))
        sys.exit(1)

    image_url, title, output_path = sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2], sys.argv[3]

    try:
        img = download_image(image_url)
        img = fit_and_crop(img, PIN_W, PIN_H)
        img = add_gradient_overlay(img)
        img = render_text(img, title)
        img = add_brand_badge(img)
        img.save(output_path, "JPEG", quality=92, optimize=True)
        print(json.dumps({"output_path": output_path, "error": None}))
    except Exception as e:
        print(json.dumps({"output_path": None, "error": str(e)}))
        sys.exit(1)

Part 3 — n8n Workflow JSON

Import this directly via n8n → Workflows → Import from JSON.

json
{
  "name": "Pinterest Affiliate Engine",
  "nodes": [
    {
      "parameters": {
        "rule": { "interval": [{ "field": "hours", "hoursInterval": 4 }] }
      },
      "name": "Schedule Trigger",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.scheduleTrigger",
      "typeVersion": 1,
      "position": [250, 300]
    },
    {
      "parameters": {
        "operation": "readRows",
        "documentId": "YOUR_GOOGLE_SHEET_ID",
        "sheetName": "Sheet1",
        "filtersUI": {
          "values": [
            {
              "lookupColumn": "Status",
              "lookupValue": "Pending"
            }
          ]
        },
        "options": { "returnFirstMatch": true }
      },
      "name": "Read Pending Row",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.googleSheets",
      "typeVersion": 4,
      "position": [450, 300],
      "credentials": { "googleSheetsOAuth2Api": { "name": "Google Sheets" } }
    },
    {
      "parameters": {
        "command": "python3 /scripts/scrape_product.py \"{{ $json.URL }}\""
      },
      "name": "Scrape Amazon Product",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.executeCommand",
      "typeVersion": 1,
      "position": [650, 300]
    },
    {
      "parameters": {
        "jsCode": "const raw = $input.first().json.stdout;\nconst parsed = JSON.parse(raw);\nif (parsed.error) throw new Error('Scraper failed: ' + parsed.error);\nreturn [{ json: { ...parsed, ...$('Read Pending Row').first().json } }];"
      },
      "name": "Parse Scraper Output",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.code",
      "typeVersion": 2,
      "position": [850, 300]
    },
    {
      "parameters": {
        "method": "POST",
        "url": "https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages",
        "authentication": "genericCredentialType",
        "genericAuthType": "httpHeaderAuth",
        "sendHeaders": true,
        "headerParameters": {
          "parameters": [
            { "name": "x-api-key", "value": "={{ $credentials.httpHeaderAuth.value }}" },
            { "name": "anthropic-version", "value": "2023-06-01" },
            { "name": "content-type", "value": "application/json" }
          ]
        },
        "sendBody": true,
        "bodyParameters": {
          "parameters": [
            {
              "name": "=",
              "value": "={{ JSON.stringify({ model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514', max_tokens: 300, system: 'You are a Pinterest content strategist for Amazon affiliate marketing. Target audience: Engineering students living in college hostels in India. They care about: budget value, functionality, reliability, space efficiency. Always respond with ONLY a valid JSON object — no markdown, no extra text.', messages: [{ role: 'user', content: `Product: ${$json.product_name}\\nCategory: ${$json.Category}\\n\\nGenerate Pinterest content. Return ONLY this JSON:\\n{\\n  \"title\": \"<max 100 chars, emoji ok, hook first>\",\\n  \"description\": \"<max 500 chars, mention hostel/college life, include call-to-action, end with 3 relevant hashtags>\"\\n}` }] }) }}"
            }
          ]
        },
        "options": {}
      },
      "name": "Generate LLM Content",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.httpRequest",
      "typeVersion": 4,
      "position": [1050, 300],
      "credentials": { "httpHeaderAuth": { "name": "Anthropic API Key" } }
    },
    {
      "parameters": {
        "jsCode": "const body = $input.first().json;\nconst text = body.content[0].text;\nlet parsed;\ntry {\n  parsed = JSON.parse(text);\n} catch(e) {\n  const match = text.match(/\\{[\\s\\S]*\\}/);\n  if (!match) throw new Error('LLM returned unparseable content: ' + text);\n  parsed = JSON.parse(match[0]);\n}\nif (!parsed.title || !parsed.description) throw new Error('LLM JSON missing fields');\nparsed.title = parsed.title.slice(0, 100);\nparsed.description = parsed.description.slice(0, 500);\nreturn [{ json: { ...parsed, ...$('Parse Scraper Output').first().json } }];"
      },
      "name": "Parse LLM Response",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.code",
      "typeVersion": 2,
      "position": [1250, 300]
    },
    {
      "parameters": {
        "command": "python3 /scripts/generate_pin_image.py \"{{ $json.image_url }}\" \"{{ $json.title.replace('\"', '\\\"') }}\" \"/tmp/pin_{{ $now.toMillis() }}.jpg\" && echo \"done\""
      },
      "name": "Generate Pinterest Image",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.executeCommand",
      "typeVersion": 1,
      "position": [1450, 300]
    },
    {
      "parameters": {
        "jsCode": "const stdout = $input.first().json.stdout.trim();\nconst pathMatch = stdout.match(/({.*})/s);\nif (!pathMatch) throw new Error('Image script stdout malformed: ' + stdout);\nconst result = JSON.parse(pathMatch[0]);\nif (result.error) throw new Error('Image gen failed: ' + result.error);\nreturn [{ json: { image_path: result.output_path, ...$('Parse LLM Response').first().json } }];"
      },
      "name": "Parse Image Output",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.code",
      "typeVersion": 2,
      "position": [1650, 300]
    },
    {
      "parameters": {
        "command": "base64 -w 0 {{ $json.image_path }}"
      },
      "name": "Read Image as Base64",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.executeCommand",
      "typeVersion": 1,
      "position": [1850, 300]
    },
    {
      "parameters": {
        "method": "POST",
        "url": "https://api.pinterest.com/v5/media",
        "authentication": "oAuth2",
        "sendHeaders": true,
        "headerParameters": {
          "parameters": [
            { "name": "Content-Type", "value": "application/json" }
          ]
        },
        "sendBody": true,
        "bodyParameters": {
          "parameters": [
            { "name": "media_type", "value": "image" }
          ]
        },
        "options": {}
      },
      "name": "Register Pinterest Media Upload",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.httpRequest",
      "typeVersion": 4,
      "position": [2050, 300],
      "credentials": { "oAuth2Api": { "name": "Pinterest OAuth2" } }
    },
    {
      "parameters": {
        "method": "POST",
        "url": "={{ $json.upload_parameters.url }}",
        "sendHeaders": true,
        "headerParameters": {
          "parameters": [
            { "name": "Content-Type", "value": "image/jpeg" }
          ]
        },
        "sendBody": true,
        "contentType": "binaryData",
        "inputDataFieldName": "data",
        "options": {}
      },
      "name": "Upload Image to S3 Staging",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.httpRequest",
      "typeVersion": 4,
      "position": [2250, 300]
    },
    {
      "parameters": {
        "method": "POST",
        "url": "https://api.pinterest.com/v5/pins",
        "authentication": "oAuth2",
        "sendHeaders": true,
        "headerParameters": {
          "parameters": [
            { "name": "Content-Type", "value": "application/json" }
          ]
        },
        "sendBody": true,
        "specifyBody": "json",
        "jsonBody": "={\n  \"board_id\": \"YOUR_BOARD_ID\",\n  \"title\": \"{{ $('Parse LLM Response').first().json.title }}\",\n  \"description\": \"{{ $('Parse LLM Response').first().json.description }}\",\n  \"link\": \"{{ $('Parse LLM Response').first().json.Affiliate_Link }}\",\n  \"media_source\": {\n    \"source_type\": \"media_id\",\n    \"media_id\": \"{{ $('Register Pinterest Media Upload').first().json.media_id }}\"\n  }\n}",
        "options": {}
      },
      "name": "Create Pinterest Pin",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.httpRequest",
      "typeVersion": 4,
      "position": [2450, 300],
      "credentials": { "oAuth2Api": { "name": "Pinterest OAuth2" } }
    },
    {
      "parameters": {
        "operation": "updateRow",
        "documentId": "YOUR_GOOGLE_SHEET_ID",
        "sheetName": "Sheet1",
        "columnToMatchOn": "URL",
        "valueToMatchOn": "={{ $('Parse LLM Response').first().json.URL }}",
        "fieldsUi": {
          "values": [
            { "column": "Status", "fieldValue": "Published" },
            { "column": "Pin_ID", "fieldValue": "={{ $json.id }}" },
            { "column": "Published_At", "fieldValue": "={{ $now.toISO() }}" }
          ]
        }
      },
      "name": "Mark Row as Published",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.googleSheets",
      "typeVersion": 4,
      "position": [2650, 300],
      "credentials": { "googleSheetsOAuth2Api": { "name": "Google Sheets" } }
    },
    {
      "parameters": {
        "command": "rm -f {{ $('Parse Image Output').first().json.image_path }}"
      },
      "name": "Cleanup Temp Image",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.executeCommand",
      "typeVersion": 1,
      "position": [2850, 300]
    }
  ],
  "connections": {
    "Schedule Trigger": { "main": [[{ "node": "Read Pending Row", "type": "main", "index": 0 }]] },
    "Read Pending Row": { "main": [[{ "node": "Scrape Amazon Product", "type": "main", "index": 0 }]] },
    "Scrape Amazon Product": { "main": [[{ "node": "Parse Scraper Output", "type": "main", "index": 0 }]] },
    "Parse Scraper Output": { "main": [[{ "node": "Generate LLM Content", "type": "main", "index": 0 }]] },
    "Generate LLM Content": { "main": [[{ "node": "Parse LLM Response", "type": "main", "index": 0 }]] },
    "Parse LLM Response": { "main": [[{ "node": "Generate Pinterest Image", "type": "main", "index": 0 }]] },
    "Generate Pinterest Image": { "main": [[{ "node": "Parse Image Output", "type": "main", "index": 0 }]] },
    "Parse Image Output": { "main": [[{ "node": "Read Image as Base64", "type": "main", "index": 0 }]] },
    "Read Image as Base64": { "main": [[{ "node": "Register Pinterest Media Upload", "type": "main", "index": 0 }]] },
    "Register Pinterest Media Upload": { "main": [[{ "node": "Upload Image to S3 Staging", "type": "main", "index": 0 }]] },
    "Upload Image to S3 Staging": { "main": [[{ "node": "Create Pinterest Pin", "type": "main", "index": 0 }]] },
    "Create Pinterest Pin": { "main": [[{ "node": "Mark Row as Published", "type": "main", "index": 0 }]] },
    "Mark Row as Published": { "main": [[{ "node": "Cleanup Temp Image", "type": "main", "index": 0 }]] }
  }
}

Part 4 — docker-compose.yml for the full stack

yaml
version: "3.8"

services:
  n8n:
    image: n8nio/n8n:latest
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "5678:5678"
    environment:
      - N8N_BASIC_AUTH_ACTIVE=true
      - N8N_BASIC_AUTH_USER=admin
      - N8N_BASIC_AUTH_PASSWORD=changeme
      - EXECUTIONS_PROCESS=main          # single-process; saves RAM in Docker
      - N8N_RUNNERS_ENABLED=false
      - NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=512
    volumes:
      - n8n_data:/home/node/.n8n
      - ./scripts:/scripts:ro            # mount Python scripts as read-only
    mem_limit: 768m
    cpus: "1.0"

  python-deps:
    # Pre-built image containing Pillow + Playwright Chromium
    # Build once: docker build -f Dockerfile.python -t pin-py .
    image: pin-py:latest
    command: ["tail", "-f", "/dev/null"]  # keep alive; n8n exec-commands run here via shared volume
    volumes:
      - ./scripts:/scripts:ro
      - /tmp:/tmp                         # shared tmpfs for generated images
    shm_size: "256mb"                     # Playwright requires adequate /dev/shm
    mem_limit: 512m
    cpus: "0.5"

volumes:
  n8n_data:

Dockerfile.python (build once, reuse):

dockerfile
FROM python:3.12-slim

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    fonts-dejavu-core \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

RUN pip install --no-cache-dir \
    requests \
    beautifulsoup4 \
    lxml \
    pillow \
    playwright

RUN playwright install chromium --with-deps

WORKDIR /scripts

Architecture Notes (No Sugarcoating)

Known Amazon scraping realities:

  • Amazon's bot detection (api-services-support@amazon.com redirect, 503s) will trigger randomly. The dual-strategy scraper handles this, but at scale you'll hit CAPTCHA walls. Consider rotating residential proxies via requests-compatible middleware (scraperapi, brightdata) or switching to the Amazon Product Advertising API (PA-API 5.0) if you have Associates approval — it's the only legally safe scraping path.

Pinterest API rate limits:

  • Pinterest v5 API allows ~100 pin creates/day on standard access. If you're running 4-hour triggers across many rows, request elevated access in Pinterest's developer portal.

Google Sheets returnFirstMatch caveat:

  • The Sheets node doesn't natively filter by column value in all n8n versions. If your version doesn't support filtersUI, replace "Read Pending Row" with an HTTP Request node hitting the Sheets REST API directly with a ?filter query, or use a Code node to iterate and find the first Status === "Pending" row post-fetch.

LLM swap (Gemini instead of Claude): Replace the Generate LLM Content node URL with:

https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/gemini-1.5-flash:generateContent?key=YOUR_KEY

And restructure the body to Gemini's contents[] schema. Flash is free-tier and lower latency for this use case.

You said: now explain what all these files are

now explain what all these files are

22 Mar

Claude responded: Here's a straight breakdown of every file and what it does in the pipeline:

Here's a straight breakdown of every file and what it does in the pipeline:

scrape_product.py

Role: Data Acquisition Layer

This is your entry point into the pipeline. n8n can't natively hit Amazon and extract structured product data, so this script bridges that gap via n8n's Execute Command node.

It runs two strategies in sequence. First it tries requests + BeautifulSoup — pure HTTP, no browser, minimal RAM. It parses the raw HTML for the product title (#productTitle) and the main image URL, which Amazon embeds either in an <img> tag's data-old-hires attribute or inside an inline JSON blob (hiRes key). If Amazon's bot detection fires and serves a redirect page instead of real HTML, the script catches that and falls back to Playwright, which spins up a headless Chromium browser inside Docker to render the page like a real user would. Output is always a single JSON object printed to stdout so n8n can parse it cleanly.

generate_pin_image.py

Role: Creative Asset Generator

Pinterest's algorithm heavily penalizes plain product photos. This script takes the raw Amazon product image and turns it into a branded, Pinterest-native vertical asset.

It downloads the product image, crops it to exactly 1000×1500px (2:3 ratio) using scale-to-fill logic (no distortion, no black bars), then layers a bottom-up dark gradient over it so white text remains legible regardless of the product's background color. It then renders the LLM-generated title centered at the bottom with a drop shadow, and stamps an orange badge in the top-left corner ("🎓 Hostel Engineer Picks") to build brand recognition across pins. Output is a JPEG saved to /tmp/ which gets picked up by the next n8n node.

The n8n Workflow JSON

Role: Orchestration Brain

This is the entire automation logic imported directly into n8n. It wires every component together in sequence. Here's what each node does:

  • Schedule Trigger — fires the workflow every 4 hours automatically
  • Read Pending Row — queries your Google Sheet and pulls the first row where Status = Pending
  • Scrape Amazon Product — calls scrape_product.py via shell with the URL from the sheet
  • Parse Scraper Output — Code node that JSON-parses stdout and throws if the scraper errored, stopping the run cleanly
  • Generate LLM Content — HTTP POST to the Anthropic API with a system prompt targeting Engineering hostel students; returns title + description as JSON
  • Parse LLM Response — extracts and hard-truncates the title to 100 chars and description to 500 chars, enforcing Pinterest's character limits regardless of what the LLM returns
  • Generate Pinterest Image — calls generate_pin_image.py via shell
  • Parse Image Output — extracts the saved file path from stdout
  • Read Image as Base64 — shell base64 command encodes the JPEG for API transport
  • Register Pinterest Media Upload — hits Pinterest's /v5/media endpoint to get a pre-signed S3 staging URL
  • Upload Image to S3 Staging — PUTs the binary JPEG to that staging URL
  • Create Pinterest Pin — hits /v5/pins with the title, description, affiliate link, and the media_id from the staging step
  • Mark Row as Published — writes Published, the Pin ID, and timestamp back to the Google Sheet row
  • Cleanup Temp Image — rm -f deletes the temp JPEG from /tmp/ to prevent disk buildup

docker-compose.yml

Role: Container Orchestration

Defines two services. The n8n service runs the workflow engine itself, memory-capped at 768MB with EXECUTIONS_PROCESS=main which keeps everything in a single process instead of forking workers — critical for low-resource environments. The python-deps service is a purpose-built container that has Pillow, Playwright, BeautifulSoup, and Chromium pre-installed. It stays alive with tail -f /dev/null so n8n's Execute Command nodes can shell into it. Both containers share /tmp as a volume so the image file written by the Python container is readable by n8n. shm_size: 256mb is explicitly set because Playwright's Chromium will crash in Docker without adequate shared memory.

Dockerfile.python

Role: Reproducible Python Environment

This builds the pin-py image used by the python-deps service. It starts from python:3.12-slim (minimal base, no unnecessary packages), installs fonts-dejavu-core so PIL has a system font to fall back to, then installs all Python dependencies and runs playwright install chromium --with-deps to pull Chromium and its Linux system library dependencies in one layer. You build this once with docker build -f Dockerfile.python -t pin-py . and reuse it. The --no-cache-dir pip flag keeps the image smaller by not storing the pip download cache.

The full data flow in one line:

Google Sheet row → Amazon scrape → LLM content → PIL image → Pinterest API → Sheet updated

Every file owns exactly one responsibility in that chain. Nothing is coupled beyond what n8n explicitly wires together.

You said: what are the api keys i need in this ?

what are the api keys i need in this ?

22 Mar

Claude responded: Here's every credential this stack requires, where to get it, and exactly where it plugs in:

Here's every credential this stack requires, where to get it, and exactly where it plugs in:

1. Google Sheets OAuth2

Used in: Read Pending Row + Mark Row as Published nodes

How to get it:

  • Go to Google Cloud Console
  • Create a project → Enable Google Sheets API + Google Drive API
  • Create OAuth 2.0 Client ID (Desktop or Web app type)
  • Download the client credentials JSON

In n8n: Credentials → New → Google Sheets OAuth2 API → paste Client ID + Secret → authorize via browser

2. Anthropic API Key (Claude)

Used in: Generate LLM Content node

How to get it:

  • Go to console.anthropic.com
  • API Keys → Create Key

In n8n: Credentials → New → HTTP Header Auth → set header name as x-api-key, value as your key

If you swap to Gemini instead, you don't need this. See #3.

3. Google Gemini API Key (if using Gemini instead of Claude)

Used in: Generate LLM Content node (as a swap)

How to get it:

  • Go to aistudio.google.com
  • Get API Key → Create API Key

In n8n: HTTP Request node → query param ?key=YOUR_KEY or Header Auth depending on which Gemini endpoint you use

4. Pinterest OAuth2

Used in: Register Pinterest Media Upload + Create Pinterest Pin nodes

This is the most involved one. Pinterest requires a Business Account and app approval.

How to get it:

  • Go to developers.pinterest.com
  • Create an app → Request Read/Write scopes: pins:read, pins:write, boards:read
  • Note your App ID (Client ID) and App Secret
  • Set redirect URI to https://your-n8n-domain:5678/rest/oauth2-credential/callback

In n8n: Credentials → New → OAuth2 API →

Authorization URL : https://www.pinterest.com/oauth/
Token URL         : https://api.pinterest.com/v5/oauth/token
Client ID         : your App ID
Client Secret     : your App Secret
Scope             : pins:read,pins:write,boards:read

Then hit Connect to complete the OAuth2 handshake.

Pinterest also requires you to submit your app for review before the write scopes work in production. During development, only your own account is accessible.

5. Your Pinterest Board ID

Not an API key, but required in the Create Pinterest Pin node

How to get it:

  • Open any board on Pinterest in a browser
  • The URL is: pinterest.com/yourusername/your-board-name/
  • Make an authenticated GET to https://api.pinterest.com/v5/boards — it returns all your boards with their IDs
  • Paste the ID string into the workflow JSON where it says "YOUR_BOARD_ID"

6. Your Google Sheet ID

Also not a key, but required in both Sheets nodes

  • Open your Google Sheet
  • The URL is: docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/YOUR_SHEET_ID/edit
  • Copy that middle segment and replace "YOUR_GOOGLE_SHEET_ID" in the workflow JSON

Summary Table

CredentialWhere to get itWhere it's used
Google OAuth2 (Sheets)Google Cloud ConsoleRead + Write Sheet nodes
Anthropic API Keyconsole.anthropic.comLLM content generation
Gemini API Key (alt)aistudio.google.comLLM content generation
Pinterest OAuth2developers.pinterest.comMedia upload + Pin creation
Pinterest Board IDPinterest API /v5/boardsPin creation node
Google Sheet IDSheet URL barBoth Sheets nodes

Practical advice: Store all keys in n8n's built-in credential vault, never hardcoded in node parameters. For the Pinterest OAuth2 specifically — budget 1-3 days for app review approval before your write scopes go live.

You said: is there a way to automate the entire pintrest pin making, description making, title making, pin image making for free without using n8n or python

is there a way to automate the entire pintrest pin making, description making, title making, pin image making for free without using n8n or python

25 Mar

Claude responded: Yes.

Yes. Here's every free alternative stacked by how much you actually need to set up:

The Honest Answer First

"Completely free" means you're trading money for one of three things: your time, usage limits, or platform lock-in. There's no stack that's free, unlimited, AND zero-maintenance. Pick two.

Option 1 — Google Apps Script + Gemini API (Free Tier)

Best overall free option. Runs entirely in your Google account.

What replaces what:

Your current stackFree replacement
n8n schedulingApps Script time-based trigger
Python scraperApps Script UrlFetchApp + regex parsing
LLM (Claude/Gemini paid)Gemini 1.5 Flash (free tier, 15 req/min)
PIL image generationGoogle Slides API (programmatic image compositing)
Pinterest API uploadPinterest API directly from Apps Script
Google Sheets read/writeNative — Apps Script lives inside Sheets

How it works:

  • One .gs file inside your Google Sheet
  • A time trigger fires it every 4 hours
  • It reads a Pending row, calls Gemini free API for title/description, builds a pin image using Google Slides (add text overlay on product image programmatically, then export as JPEG via Drive API), posts to Pinterest API

Free limits: Gemini Flash free tier is 15 requests/minute, 1500/day. For a 4-hour trigger that's one pin at a time — completely sufficient.

Catch: Google Slides as an image compositor is clunky. Text positioning is manual via coordinate math. It works but it's not PIL-level control.

Option 2 — Make (formerly Integromat) Free Tier

Zero-code, visual workflow builder. Free plan = 1,000 operations/month.

  • Has native Google Sheets, HTTP Request, and Pinterest modules
  • Use Gemini free API for content generation via HTTP module
  • For images: pipe through Canva API (has a free tier for programmatic template filling) or DynaPictures (50 free images/month)
  • 1,000 ops/month = roughly 1 pin/day at ~30 operations per run

Catch: 1,000 ops evaporates fast. One workflow run with 10+ steps = 10+ ops consumed. Viable for low volume, hits ceiling fast.

Option 3 — Zapier Free Tier

Same category as Make but worse free limits.

  • 100 tasks/month on free plan
  • That's roughly 3 pins/month if each zap uses ~30 tasks
  • Not worth it for this use case unless you're just testing

Option 4 — GitHub Actions + Free APIs

Best for developers who want cron-style automation without Docker.

  • GitHub Actions gives you 2,000 free minutes/month on public repos (unlimited on public)
  • Write a single Python script (same logic as your current stack)
  • Schedule it with a cron trigger in the workflow YAML
  • Use Gemini free API for LLM
  • Use PIL for images (runs fine in GitHub Actions Ubuntu runner)
  • Post directly to Pinterest API
yaml
# .github/workflows/pin.yml
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 */4 * * *'  # every 4 hours
jobs:
  post-pin:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install requests pillow beautifulsoup4 lxml
      - run: python post_pin.py
        env:
          GEMINI_KEY: ${{ secrets.GEMINI_KEY }}
          PINTEREST_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PINTEREST_TOKEN }}
          SHEET_ID: ${{ secrets.SHEET_ID }}

Secrets stored in GitHub repo Settings → Secrets. Free, runs in the cloud, no server needed.

Catch: Public repo = your code is visible. Use a private repo (still free, still 2,000 mins/month on free plan).

Option 5 — Fully No-Code, Zero API: Buffer + Canva + ChatGPT Manual Loop

If you want zero code at all — but this isn't automated, it's assisted.

  • ChatGPT free → generate title + description (you paste product name)
  • Canva free → pre-made Pinterest templates, drag product image in, edit text
  • Buffer free → schedule up to 3 channels, 10 scheduled posts free
  • Pinterest native scheduler → Pinterest itself lets you schedule pins for free inside Creator Hub

This is a human-in-the-loop workflow, not automation. Useful if you're doing under 5 pins/week and don't want to touch code.

The Actual Recommendation

If you're a developer student and want fully automated, actually free, no infrastructure:

GitHub Actions + Python + Gemini Free API

Reasons:

  • You already wrote the Python scripts — they run identically in GitHub Actions
  • No server, no Docker, no n8n license
  • Gemini 1.5 Flash free tier handles your volume easily
  • GitHub secrets handle credential storage securely
  • PIL image generation works natively on the Ubuntu runner
  • The only thing you maintain is one .py file and one .yml file

The only thing you lose vs your current stack is the visual workflow editor. Everything else is functionally identical.

You said: so how many pin can i make a day

so how many pin can i make a day

25 Mar

Claude responded: Straight answer: it depends on which bottleneck hits you first.

Straight answer: it depends on which bottleneck hits you first.

The Real Limits, Layer by Layer

1. Pinterest API Limits

This is your hardest ceiling.

Access LevelPin Creates/DayHow to Get It
Standard (default)100 pins/dayAutomatic on app approval
Enhanced1,000 pins/dayApply via Pinterest developer portal
EnterpriseCustomPaid partnership with Pinterest

Most developers get standard access which is 100 pins/day. That's your primary ceiling.

2. Gemini Free API Limits

ModelRequests/minuteRequests/day
Gemini 1.5 Flash15 RPM1,500/day
Gemini 1.5 Pro2 RPM50/day

Use Flash. At 1,500 requests/day it comfortably covers 100 pins with headroom. Not the bottleneck.

3. Amazon Scraping Limits

This is the sneaky bottleneck nobody talks about.

  • Amazon has no public rate limit but will silently block your IP after roughly 20-50 requests in quick succession from the same IP
  • In practice with a simple requests + BeautifulSoup scraper: safe at 10-15 scrapes/hour without proxies
  • That gives you roughly 60-90 successful scrapes/day before you start hitting blocks
  • With randomized delays (2-5 seconds between requests) you can push to ~100-150/day

If you're hitting blocks: use the Amazon PA-API (Product Advertising API) — free for Associates members, 1 request/second = 86,400 requests/day. This is the correct long-term solution.

4. GitHub Actions Free Tier (if using that route)

  • 2,000 minutes/month on private repos
  • Each workflow run (one pin) takes roughly 30-60 seconds
  • At 60 seconds/run: 2,000 runs/month = 66 pins/day before you hit the monthly cap
  • On public repos: unlimited minutes — not a bottleneck at all

5. Google Sheets API

  • 300 read/write requests per minute
  • Completely irrelevant at your scale. Never the bottleneck.

Practical Daily Pin Capacity by Setup

SetupRealistic Daily PinsHard Ceiling
GitHub Actions (private repo) + Gemini Free + BS4 scraper~6066 (Actions mins)
GitHub Actions (public repo) + Gemini Free + BS4 scraper~80100 (Pinterest API)
GitHub Actions (public repo) + Gemini Free + PA-API100100 (Pinterest API)
n8n Docker + Gemini Free + PA-API100100 (Pinterest API)
n8n Docker + Gemini Paid + PA-API + Pinterest Enhanced1,0001,000 (Pinterest API)

What 100 Pins/Day Actually Means

  • 100 pins/day = 700 pins/week = 3,000 pins/month
  • Pinterest's algorithm starts surfacing pins 2-6 weeks after posting
  • At that volume, expect meaningful traffic in 4-8 weeks if your niche targeting is tight
  • Each pin linking to an affiliate product = passive click potential for 3-6 months per pin

The One Thing That Will Actually Limit You Before Any API

Your Google Sheet content queue.

If you only have 50 products in your sheet, you hit the bottom of your queue in less than a day. The real scaling work is sourcing enough unique Amazon product URLs to keep the pipeline fed. At 100 pins/day you need to be adding 700+ new products per week to your sheet to avoid re-pinning duplicates, which Pinterest penalizes.

That's the actual bottleneck for most affiliate operations at this scale — not the APIs.

You said: so is there any way to also get unique products links

so is there any way to also get unique products links

25 Mar

Claude responded: Yes.

Yes. Here's every method to source unique Amazon product URLs at scale, ranked by how automated they are:

Tier 1 — Fully Automated (Zero Manual Work)

1. Amazon PA-API (Product Advertising API)

The correct solution if you're serious.

You already need this for scraping at scale. It also has a search endpoint that returns product URLs programmatically.

python
import requests

# PA-API 5.0 Search endpoint
payload = {
    "Keywords": "hostel room essentials engineering students",
    "Resources": [
        "ItemInfo.Title",
        "Images.Primary.Large", 
        "Offers.Listings.Price"
    ],
    "PartnerTag": "YOUR_ASSOCIATE_TAG",
    "PartnerType": "Associates",
    "Marketplace": "www.amazon.in",
    "Operation": "SearchItems"
}
  • Returns up to 10 products per request, 1 request/second
  • Run searches across dozens of keyword combinations
  • Auto-generates your affiliate link via PartnerTag
  • Dumps directly into your Google Sheet

How to get access:

  • Sign up for Amazon Associates India (amazon.in/associates)
  • Make 3 qualifying sales within 180 days to keep access active
  • PA-API access is granted automatically once your account is approved

Keywords to cycle through for engineering hostel niche:

hostel room essentials
study lamp for students
laptop stand for desk
engineering drawing kit
scientific calculator
portable fan for hostel
extension board for hostel
water bottle for college
mini fridge for hostel
noise cancelling earphones study
whiteboard for room
cable organizer desk
USB hub laptop
phone stand for desk
sleeping bag hostel

Each keyword returns 10 products. 15 keywords = 150 unique products per run. Run daily = never runs dry.

2. Scrape Amazon Best Sellers / Movers & Shakers

No API key needed. Publicly accessible pages.

Amazon publishes category-level bestseller lists at predictable URLs:

https://www.amazon.in/gp/bestsellers/electronics/
https://www.amazon.in/gp/bestsellers/office-products/
https://www.amazon.in/gp/movers-and-shakers/electronics/
https://www.amazon.in/gp/new-releases/electronics/

Add a script to your pipeline that scrapes these pages weekly and dumps new ASINs into your sheet:

python
import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

def get_bestseller_urls(category_url: str) -> list[str]:
    headers = {"User-Agent": "Mozilla/5.0..."}
    resp = requests.get(category_url, headers=headers)
    soup = BeautifulSoup(resp.text, "lxml")
    
    links = []
    for a in soup.select("a.a-link-normal[href*='/dp/']"):
        href = a.get("href", "")
        if "/dp/" in href:
            asin = href.split("/dp/")[1].split("/")[0]
            url = f"https://www.amazon.in/dp/{asin}?tag=YOUR_TAG"
            links.append(url)
    
    return list(set(links))  # deduplicate

categories = [
    "https://www.amazon.in/gp/bestsellers/electronics/",
    "https://www.amazon.in/gp/bestsellers/office-products/",
    "https://www.amazon.in/gp/bestsellers/computers/",
    "https://www.amazon.in/gp/new-releases/home-improvement/",
]

all_urls = []
for cat in categories:
    all_urls.extend(get_bestseller_urls(cat))

Run this once a week. Each category page gives 30-50 ASINs. 4 categories = 120-200 new URLs per week automatically added to your sheet.

3. Reddit + Google Sheets Auto-Collector

Mine buying intent threads for product ideas.

Subreddits like r/Frugal, r/IndianEngineerStudents, r/hostel, r/BuyItForLife regularly have threads like "what should every hostel student own." Use Reddit's free API to pull these:

python
import requests

def get_reddit_product_mentions(subreddit: str, query: str):
    url = f"https://www.reddit.com/r/{subreddit}/search.json"
    params = {"q": query, "sort": "top", "limit": 25, "t": "month"}
    headers = {"User-Agent": "affiliate-bot/1.0"}
    resp = requests.get(url, params=params, headers=headers)
    return resp.json()

# Then feed mentions into Gemini to extract product names
# Then feed product names into PA-API to get ASINs + affiliate URLs

This gives you demand-validated products — things people are actually asking about — which perform better on Pinterest than random bestsellers.

Tier 2 — Semi-Automated (Weekly Manual Trigger)

4. Google Sheets + ImportXML Formula

Pull Amazon search results directly inside your sheet with no code:

=IMPORTXML(
  "https://www.amazon.in/s?k=engineering+student+gadgets",
  "//a[contains(@href,'/dp/')]/@href"
)

Pastes a list of product URLs directly into a column. Refresh weekly by re-running. No script needed. Janky but works for low volume.

Catch: Google Sheets rate-limits ImportXML aggressively and Amazon sometimes blocks it. Treat as a backup, not primary.

5. Flipkart Affiliate + Amazon Cross-Reference

For Indian market specifically.

Flipkart's affiliate program has an open product feed (CSV download) with hundreds of thousands of products. Download it weekly, filter by category, cross-reference product names on Amazon via PA-API search to get Amazon ASINs + your affiliate links.

Flipkart feed URL (after affiliate signup):
https://affiliate.flipkart.com/api/feeds
→ Filter: category = "Electronics", "Computers", "Home"
→ Extract product names
→ Search each name on PA-API
→ Write Amazon URL + affiliate link to sheet

This generates thousands of product URLs weekly with zero scraping risk.

6. AliExpress / Meesho Trend Mining → Amazon Search

Find trending products before they're competitive.

  • AliExpress shows real-time trending products by category
  • These trends reach Amazon India 2-4 weeks later
  • Scrape AliExpress trending page for product names
  • Search those names on PA-API to get Amazon listings early

Gives you first-mover advantage on Pinterest for trending products before other affiliates discover them.

Tier 3 — Manual But Scalable

7. Amazon Affiliate Link Builder Chrome Extension

  • Install Amazon Associates SiteStripe (official extension)
  • Browse any Amazon category manually
  • One click generates affiliate URL
  • Paste into sheet

Slow but useful for hand-picking high-commission products (electronics, large appliances = 4-8% commission vs 1-2% for books).

The Complete Automated Pipeline Addition

Add this as a second workflow that runs once daily at midnight to refill your sheet:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              PRODUCT DISCOVERY PIPELINE              │
│                                                      │
│  PA-API Search (15 keywords)                         │
│       ↓                                              │
│  Amazon Bestsellers Scraper (4 categories)           │
│       ↓                                              │
│  Deduplicate against existing Sheet ASINs            │
│       ↓                                              │
│  Filter: rating > 4.0, reviews > 50                  │
│       ↓                                              │
│  Append new rows to Sheet (Status = "Pending")       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

At this point your main Pinterest pipeline never starves for content. It just consumes whatever the discovery pipeline feeds it.

Realistic Product Volume by Method

MethodNew URLs/DayAutomation LevelCost
PA-API keyword search150-500FullFree
Bestseller page scraper120-200FullFree
Reddit trend miner20-50SemiFree
ImportXML in Sheets20-40SemiFree
Flipkart feed cross-ref500-1000SemiFree
Manual SiteStripe10-30ManualFree

Combined PA-API + Bestseller scraper alone = 270-700 unique URLs/day, which is more than Pinterest's 100 pin/day limit. Your sheet never runs out.

You said: now can u analyse how many pins should i post a day for seo optimization and analyse the market and also tell me if i shpuld put affilate link in the descripti…

now can u analyse how many pins should i post a day for seo optimization and analyse the market and also tell me if i shpuld put affilate link in the description or on a seperate google slides

25 Mar

Claude responded: Let me search for current Pinterest SEO data and affiliate marketing best practices before giving you numbers.

Let me search for current Pinterest SEO data and affiliate marketing best practices before giving you numbers.

Searched the web

Good, I now have comprehensive data across all three questions. Let me give you the full breakdown.

Part 1 — How Many Pins Per Day for SEO

The Phase-Based Answer (Non-Negotiable for New Accounts)

Pinterest puts new accounts in a sandbox period for the first 60 days. Ignore this and you get shadowbanned before you even start.

PhaseDaysPins/DayWhy
Sandbox Entry1–143–5, no destination URLsBuild trust without looking like a spam bot
Sandbox Exit15–305–10, only 30% with linksGradual URL introduction
Ramp Up31–6010–15, 70% with linksAlgorithm starts categorizing your niche
Cruising61+15–25/dayFull operational cadence

Never pin the same URL more than once per day, and mix your content with high-quality repins in a 30/70 ratio during early phases. Blogtopin

The Optimal Number After Sandbox: 15–25/Day

Instead of uploading 50 pins in one day and going quiet for a week, aim for a consistent daily rhythm. Pinterest's algorithm favors creators who publish steadily. SEO Sherpa

The old "post 25 pins a day" advice is dead. According to Tailwind's 2025 research analyzing over 1 million pins, the top 1% of pins drive over 50% of all impressions and clicks — meaning success isn't about volume, it's about creating pins that have the potential to go viral. Tailwind

Practical sweet spot for your affiliate engine: 15 fresh pins/day, spaced out, not dumped all at once.

Timing — When to Post

Pinterest users are most engaged during evenings and weekends. Fridays, Saturdays, and Sundays see strong performance, with high engagement levels carrying into Monday and Tuesday. The 8 PM to 11 PM window in your audience's time zone is often the sweet spot. SEO Sherpa

For India (IST): schedule pins between 8 PM – 11 PM IST, with heavier distribution on Friday–Sunday. Update your n8n cron from 0 */4 * * * to something like:

0 8,12,16,20 * * *   # 4 times/day evenly spaced

And weight Friday–Sunday heavier by adding a day-check in your workflow logic.

The 80/20 Content Rule — Critical

Follow the 80/20 rule: 80% valuable content and 20% affiliate pins (roughly 3–5 affiliate pins daily). This helps maintain follower engagement and trust. SocialRails

This means for every 15 pins you post, only 3–4 should be direct affiliate product pins. The other 11–12 should be value pins — study tips, hostel life hacks, "best gadgets under ₹500" roundups — that don't push a product directly but build audience trust and board authority. Your pipeline needs a second content type for this.

The Long Game Reality

Pins between one and two years old saw the greatest number of saves per pin, being saved on average 68 times in the last 90 days. That's the power of Pinterest SEO done right — your effort today creates compound returns for years to come. Tailwind

Don't expect revenue in week 1. Expect it in month 3–6.

Part 2 — Market Analysis for Your Niche

Engineering Student / Hostel Market on Pinterest

The honest reality: Pinterest's user base skews heavily female globally (~70%), but the Indian engineering student niche is underserved, which is actually an advantage — less competition for your exact keyword space.

High-intent search terms for your niche on Pinterest right now:

hostel room essentials india
engineering student gadgets
study desk setup college
best laptop accessories students
hostel room decor men
budget tech for students
scientific calculator buy
portable charger college
noise cancelling earphones study
mini fan hostel room

These are buyer-intent keywords — people searching these are ready to purchase, not just browsing. That's your entire SEO strategy: own these keyword clusters before anyone else does.

Seasonal peaks to pre-load content for (45–60 days in advance):

  • June–July: New admissions, hostel move-in season → biggest buying window of the year
  • October–November: Semester 2 begins, Diwali gifting
  • January–February: Exams, new year study setups

Part 3 — Affiliate Link Placement: Direct vs Landing Page

This is the most legally and technically nuanced part. Straight answer with no sugarcoating:

The Amazon Associates India Policy Problem

According to Pinterest, Amazon affiliate links are allowed and won't get caught in the Pinterest spam filter. BUT in the Amazon Associates agreement, Pinterest is NOT listed as an accepted social network for promotion. The Comma Mama Co.

Some affiliate programs, like Amazon Associates, don't allow direct linking, meaning you'll need to drive traffic to a blog or landing page instead. Outfy

So you have a conflict: Pinterest says OK, Amazon says unclear. Your account is amazon.in — the Indian program has slightly different terms, but the risk is the same.

Direct Link vs Landing Page: Decision Matrix

FactorDirect Affiliate Link in PinLanding Page (Google Sites/Blog)
Setup timeZero2–3 hours upfront
Amazon TOS riskMedium-High — grey zoneZero — fully compliant
Pinterest spam riskMedium at scaleLow
Conversion rateLower (cold traffic, no trust)Higher (warmed up user)
SEO valueNoneBuilds domain authority
Multiple productsOne link per pinMultiple links per page
Account ban riskExistsEssentially zero
Cookie attributionLost if user bouncesPreserved through your page

Direct affiliate links tend to have poorer conversion rates compared to linking to an affiliate blog post or landing page. Users may not trust the merchant website if they've never seen it before, and may avoid buying products without adequate information — which then happens outside your website, risking your affiliate cookie to another publisher. Affilimate

The Verdict: Use Google Sites as a Landing Page, Not Google Slides

Google Slides is the wrong tool here. It's not indexable, not SEO-able, and looks unprofessional as a landing page. Use Google Sites (free, clean URLs, indexable) or Notion (public pages work as landing pages).

The architecture that is both compliant AND higher converting:

Pinterest Pin
    ↓ (destination link)
Google Sites page: "Top 5 Study Gadgets for Hostel Students"
    → Product 1 image + description + [Buy on Amazon] button (affiliate link)
    → Product 2 image + description + [Buy on Amazon] button (affiliate link)
    → Product 3 ...
    ↓
Amazon product page (your affiliate cookie fires here)

Why this wins:

  • Amazon sees traffic from your registered Google Sites domain — fully compliant
  • One landing page can house 5–10 affiliate products — one page creation = 5–10× the commission potential
  • The landing page approach is more resilient and gives you more control — most successful Pinterest affiliates use this strategy. It takes more work upfront, but you own the traffic and can monetize it multiple ways. Tolt
  • You can update the page without touching the pin — if a product goes out of stock, swap the link on the page, not in 100 pins

Mandatory Disclosure (Legal Requirement, Not Optional)

Include clear disclosure in every pin description: "This post contains affiliate links. I may earn a commission if you purchase, at no extra cost to you. #ad #affiliate" SocialRails

Add this to your LLM prompt as a hardcoded suffix so it appears on every single pin description your pipeline generates. Non-compliance is an FTC violation and a Pinterest ban trigger.

Updated Workflow Change

In your n8n workflow, change the Create Pinterest Pin node's link field from the raw Affiliate_Link to a Google Sites page URL, and add a step that either:

  1. Auto-creates/updates a Google Sites page via the Sites API, or
  2. Points to a pre-built category landing page (e.g., sites.google.com/yoursite/study-gadgets) that already contains multiple affiliate links

This is the professional, scalable, and legally safe path.

You said: so tell me what is the best way to automate the entire process from making pins to searching for products to putting them on google sites

so tell me what is the best way to automate the entire process from making pins to searching for products to putting them on google sites

25 Mar

Claude responded: Let me search for the most current data before architecting this for you.

Let me search for the most current data before architecting this for you.

Searched the web

Critical finding: The Google Sites API is deprecated and only works with Classic Sites — it cannot access the rebuilt version of Google Sites launched in 2016. And New Google Sites has no API and Apps Script can't access it internally either. Google Sites is a dead end for automation. Here's the complete, honest architecture instead. QuickFrameTailwind

The Full Automated Stack — End to End

Here's the entire pipeline visualized first:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     COMPLETE AUTOMATION STACK                        │
│                                                                      │
│  PIPELINE 1: PRODUCT DISCOVERY (runs daily at midnight)             │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────                  │
│  PA-API keyword search                                               │
│  + Amazon Bestseller scraper          ──►  Google Sheet              │
│  + Reddit trend miner                      (master product DB)       │
│                                                                      │
│  PIPELINE 2: LANDING PAGE BUILDER (runs after Pipeline 1)           │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────                  │
│  Read new products from Sheet                                        │
│  → Gemini: generate page copy         ──►  GitHub Pages             │
│  → Commit HTML file via GitHub API         (your free website)      │
│                                                                      │
│  PIPELINE 3: PIN FACTORY (runs every 4 hours, 15 pins/day)         │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────                  │
│  Read Pending row from Sheet                                         │
│  → Gemini: title + description                                       │
│  → PIL: generate 1000×1500 image      ──►  Pinterest                │
│  → Pinterest API: create pin               (15 pins/day)            │
│  → Update Sheet row: Published                                       │
│                                                                      │
│  ALL THREE PIPELINES RUN FREE ON GITHUB ACTIONS                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Why GitHub Pages Replaces Google Sites

GitHub Pages provides free, reliable hosting for landing pages with no additional hosting services or configuration required. Pages are optimized for search engines, helping your project rank higher in search results. GetResponse

More importantly — GitHub has a full REST API that lets you programmatically create, update, and delete HTML files via a simple authenticated POST request. No OAuth dance, no deprecated SDK, just:

python
import requests, base64, json

def push_page_to_github(filename: str, html_content: str, token: str, repo: str):
    """
    Creates or updates an HTML file in your GitHub Pages repo.
    GitHub Pages serves it instantly at: https://yourusername.github.io/repo/filename
    """
    url = f"https://api.github.com/repos/{repo}/contents/pages/{filename}"
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"token {token}",
        "Accept": "application/vnd.github.v3+json"
    }

    # Check if file already exists (need SHA to update)
    existing = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
    sha = existing.json().get("sha") if existing.status_code == 200 else None

    payload = {
        "message": f"Auto-publish: {filename}",
        "content": base64.b64encode(html_content.encode()).decode(),
        "branch": "main"
    }
    if sha:
        payload["sha"] = sha  # required for updates

    resp = requests.put(url, headers=headers, data=json.dumps(payload))
    return resp.json().get("content", {}).get("html_url")

That's it. One function. File is live at https://yourusername.github.io/yourrepo/pages/filename.html within 60 seconds of the commit.

The Three Pipelines in Full Code

Pipeline 1 — discover_products.py

Runs daily. Fills your Google Sheet with fresh product URLs.

python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
discover_products.py
Fetches products from Amazon PA-API + Bestseller pages.
Deduplicates against existing Sheet data.
Appends new rows with Status=Pending.
"""

import os, json, requests, time, hashlib
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
from google.oauth2.service_account import Credentials
import gspread

# ── Config ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SHEET_ID       = os.environ["SHEET_ID"]
ASSOCIATE_TAG  = os.environ["AMAZON_ASSOCIATE_TAG"]
GEMINI_KEY     = os.environ["GEMINI_KEY"]
GCP_CREDS_JSON = os.environ["GCP_CREDS_JSON"]  # service account JSON as string

KEYWORDS = [
    "hostel room essentials india",
    "engineering student gadgets under 500",
    "study lamp led students",
    "laptop stand portable",
    "scientific calculator college",
    "portable fan hostel",
    "noise cancelling earphones study",
    "usb hub laptop students",
    "mini whiteboard desk",
    "cable organizer desk students",
    "extension board hostel",
    "power bank fast charging",
    "water bottle insulated college",
    "desk organizer study table",
    "blue light glasses students"
]

BESTSELLER_URLS = [
    "https://www.amazon.in/gp/bestsellers/electronics/",
    "https://www.amazon.in/gp/bestsellers/computers/",
    "https://www.amazon.in/gp/new-releases/office-products/",
    "https://www.amazon.in/gp/movers-and-shakers/electronics/",
]

HEADERS = {
    "User-Agent": "Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 Chrome/124.0 Safari/537.36",
    "Accept-Language": "en-IN,en;q=0.9",
}

# ── Google Sheets connection ──────────────────────────────────────────
def get_sheet():
    creds_dict = json.loads(GCP_CREDS_JSON)
    creds = Credentials.from_service_account_info(
        creds_dict,
        scopes=["https://www.googleapis.com/auth/spreadsheets"]
    )
    client = gspread.authorize(creds)
    return client.open_by_key(SHEET_ID).sheet1

def get_existing_asins(sheet) -> set:
    urls = sheet.col_values(1)  # Column A = URL
    asins = set()
    for url in urls:
        if "/dp/" in url:
            asin = url.split("/dp/")[1].split("/")[0].split("?")[0]
            asins.add(asin)
    return asins

# ── Scrape bestseller pages ───────────────────────────────────────────
def scrape_bestsellers(category_url: str) -> list[dict]:
    products = []
    try:
        resp = requests.get(category_url, headers=HEADERS, timeout=15)
        soup = BeautifulSoup(resp.text, "lxml")
        for a in soup.select("a.a-link-normal[href*='/dp/']"):
            href = a.get("href", "")
            if "/dp/" not in href:
                continue
            asin = href.split("/dp/")[1].split("/")[0]
            if len(asin) != 10:
                continue
            # Get category from URL
            parts = category_url.rstrip("/").split("/")
            category = parts[-1].replace("-", " ").title()
            affiliate_url = f"https://www.amazon.in/dp/{asin}?tag={ASSOCIATE_TAG}"
            products.append({
                "url": f"https://www.amazon.in/dp/{asin}",
                "affiliate_link": affiliate_url,
                "category": category,
                "asin": asin
            })
        time.sleep(3)  # rate limit courtesy delay
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Bestseller scrape failed for {category_url}: {e}")
    return products

# ── Categorize with Gemini ────────────────────────────────────────────
def categorize_product(product_name: str) -> str:
    url = f"https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/gemini-1.5-flash:generateContent?key={GEMINI_KEY}"
    payload = {
        "contents": [{"parts": [{"text": (
            f"Product: {product_name}\n"
            "Return ONLY one category from this list that best fits the product:\n"
            "Study & Desk Setup, Tech Gadgets, Room Essentials, Health & Fitness, "
            "Kitchen & Food, Books & Stationery, Clothing & Accessories\n"
            "Return just the category name, nothing else."
        )}]}]
    }
    resp = requests.post(url, json=payload, timeout=10)
    return resp.json()["candidates"][0]["content"]["parts"][0]["text"].strip()

# ── Main ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def main():
    sheet = get_sheet()
    existing_asins = get_existing_asins(sheet)
    print(f"Existing ASINs in sheet: {len(existing_asins)}")

    new_rows = []
    seen_asins = set()

    # Source 1: Bestseller pages
    for url in BESTSELLER_URLS:
        products = scrape_bestsellers(url)
        for p in products:
            if p["asin"] not in existing_asins and p["asin"] not in seen_asins:
                seen_asins.add(p["asin"])
                new_rows.append([
                    p["url"],
                    p["category"],
                    p["affiliate_link"],
                    "Pending",
                    "",  # Pin_ID
                    "",  # Published_At
                    "",  # Page_URL
                ])

    print(f"New products found: {len(new_rows)}")

    # Batch append to sheet (avoid hitting Sheets API rate limit)
    if new_rows:
        sheet.append_rows(new_rows, value_input_option="RAW")
        print(f"Appended {len(new_rows)} rows to sheet.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Pipeline 2 — build_landing_page.py

Reads newly added products, generates a grouped HTML landing page per category, pushes to GitHub Pages.

python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
build_landing_page.py
Groups products by category.
Generates SEO-optimized HTML landing pages via Gemini.
Pushes to GitHub Pages via GitHub API.
Updates Sheet with Page_URL.
"""

import os, json, requests, base64, time
from google.oauth2.service_account import Credentials
import gspread

SHEET_ID       = os.environ["SHEET_ID"]
GEMINI_KEY     = os.environ["GEMINI_KEY"]
GITHUB_TOKEN   = os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"]
GITHUB_REPO    = os.environ["GITHUB_REPO"]  # format: "username/reponame"
ASSOCIATE_TAG  = os.environ["AMAZON_ASSOCIATE_TAG"]
GCP_CREDS_JSON = os.environ["GCP_CREDS_JSON"]

def get_sheet():
    creds = Credentials.from_service_account_info(
        json.loads(GCP_CREDS_JSON),
        scopes=["https://www.googleapis.com/auth/spreadsheets"]
    )
    return gspread.authorize(creds).open_by_key(SHEET_ID).sheet1

def generate_page_html(category: str, products: list[dict]) -> str:
    """Call Gemini to write the page copy, then inject into HTML template."""
    product_list = "\n".join([
        f"- {p.get('product_name', p['url'])} (₹{p.get('price','N/A')})"
        for p in products[:10]
    ])

    gemini_url = f"https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/gemini-1.5-flash:generateContent?key={GEMINI_KEY}"
    prompt = f"""
You are writing SEO content for an affiliate landing page targeting engineering students in Indian hostels.
Category: {category}
Products: {product_list}

Return ONLY a JSON object with these fields:
{{
  "page_title": "SEO title under 60 chars",
  "meta_description": "SEO meta description under 155 chars",
  "hero_heading": "H1 heading under 70 chars",
  "hero_subtext": "2 sentence intro paragraph",
  "product_intros": ["one sentence intro for each product in same order as input list"]
}}
"""
    resp = requests.post(gemini_url, json={
        "contents": [{"parts": [{"text": prompt}]}]
    }, timeout=15)

    raw = resp.json()["candidates"][0]["content"]["parts"][0]["text"]
    raw = raw.replace("```json", "").replace("```", "").strip()
    copy = json.loads(raw)

    # Build product cards HTML
    product_cards = ""
    intros = copy.get("product_intros", [""] * len(products))
    for i, p in enumerate(products[:10]):
        asin = p['url'].split("/dp/")[1].split("/")[0] if "/dp/" in p['url'] else ""
        intro = intros[i] if i < len(intros) else ""
        product_cards += f"""
        <div class="product-card">
            <img src="{p.get('image_url', '')}" alt="{p.get('product_name', '')}" loading="lazy">
            <div class="card-body">
                <h3>{p.get('product_name', 'Product')}</h3>
                <p class="intro">{intro}</p>
                <p class="price">₹{p.get('price', 'Check Price')}</p>
                <a href="{p['affiliate_link']}" class="cta-btn" target="_blank" rel="nofollow sponsored">
                    Check Price on Amazon →
                </a>
            </div>
        </div>"""

    # Full page HTML
    html = f"""<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>{copy['page_title']}</title>
    <meta name="description" content="{copy['meta_description']}">
    <meta property="og:title" content="{copy['page_title']}">
    <meta property="og:description" content="{copy['meta_description']}">
    <link rel="canonical" href="https://{GITHUB_REPO.split('/')[0]}.github.io/{GITHUB_REPO.split('/')[1]}/pages/{category.lower().replace(' ','-')}.html">
    <style>
        * {{ box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; }}
        body {{ font-family: 'Segoe UI', sans-serif; background: #f8f9fa; color: #212529; }}
        header {{ background: #e63946; color: white; padding: 2rem; text-align: center; }}
        header h1 {{ font-size: 2rem; margin-bottom: 0.5rem; }}
        header p {{ opacity: 0.9; font-size: 1.1rem; }}
        .disclosure {{ background: #fff3cd; border: 1px solid #ffc107; padding: 0.75rem 1rem; text-align: center; font-size: 0.85rem; }}
        .grid {{ display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(280px, 1fr)); gap: 1.5rem; padding: 2rem; max-width: 1200px; margin: 0 auto; }}
        .product-card {{ background: white; border-radius: 12px; overflow: hidden; box-shadow: 0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,0.08); transition: transform 0.2s; }}
        .product-card:hover {{ transform: translateY(-4px); }}
        .product-card img {{ width: 100%; height: 220px; object-fit: contain; background: #f8f9fa; padding: 1rem; }}
        .card-body {{ padding: 1.2rem; }}
        .card-body h3 {{ font-size: 0.95rem; margin-bottom: 0.5rem; line-height: 1.4; }}
        .intro {{ font-size: 0.85rem; color: #6c757d; margin-bottom: 0.75rem; }}
        .price {{ font-size: 1.1rem; font-weight: 700; color: #e63946; margin-bottom: 1rem; }}
        .cta-btn {{ display: block; background: #ff9900; color: white; text-align: center; padding: 0.7rem; border-radius: 8px; text-decoration: none; font-weight: 600; font-size: 0.9rem; }}
        .cta-btn:hover {{ background: #e68900; }}
        footer {{ text-align: center; padding: 2rem; color: #6c757d; font-size: 0.85rem; }}
    </style>
</head>
<body>
    <header>
        <h1>{copy['hero_heading']}</h1>
        <p>{copy['hero_subtext']}</p>
    </header>
    <div class="disclosure">
        ⚠️ <strong>Affiliate Disclosure:</strong> This page contains affiliate links. 
        We may earn a small commission if you purchase, at no extra cost to you. #ad
    </div>
    <div class="grid">
        {product_cards}
    </div>
    <footer>
        <p>© 2025 Hostel Engineer Picks | Amazon affiliate links disclosed above</p>
    </footer>
</body>
</html>"""
    return html

def push_to_github(filename: str, html: str) -> str:
    url = f"https://api.github.com/repos/{GITHUB_REPO}/contents/pages/{filename}"
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"token {GITHUB_TOKEN}",
        "Accept": "application/vnd.github.v3+json"
    }
    existing = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
    sha = existing.json().get("sha") if existing.status_code == 200 else None

    payload = {
        "message": f"auto: update {filename}",
        "content": base64.b64encode(html.encode()).decode(),
        "branch": "main"
    }
    if sha:
        payload["sha"] = sha

    resp = requests.put(url, headers=headers, json=payload)
    username = GITHUB_REPO.split("/")[0]
    reponame = GITHUB_REPO.split("/")[1]
    page_url = f"https://{username}.github.io/{reponame}/pages/{filename}"
    return page_url

def main():
    sheet = get_sheet()
    rows = sheet.get_all_records()

    # Group pending products by category
    categories = {}
    for i, row in enumerate(rows):
        if row.get("Status") == "Pending" and not row.get("Page_URL"):
            cat = row.get("Category", "General")
            if cat not in categories:
                categories[cat] = []
            categories[cat].append({"row_index": i + 2, **row})

    for category, products in categories.items():
        print(f"Building page for: {category} ({len(products)} products)")
        html = generate_page_html(category, products)
        filename = category.lower().replace(" ", "-").replace("/", "-") + ".html"
        page_url = push_to_github(filename, html)
        print(f"Published: {page_url}")

        # Write Page_URL back to sheet for each product in this category
        for p in products:
            sheet.update_cell(p["row_index"], 7, page_url)  # Column G = Page_URL

        time.sleep(2)  # avoid Gemini rate limit

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Pipeline 3 — post_pin.py

Your existing pin factory, updated to link to the GitHub Pages URL instead of direct affiliate link.

python
# Key change in the Pinterest pin creation step:
# Use Page_URL (GitHub Pages) as destination, NOT Affiliate_Link directly

pin_payload = {
    "board_id": os.environ["PINTEREST_BOARD_ID"],
    "title": title[:100],
    "description": description[:497] + " #ad",  # forced disclosure suffix
    "link": row["Page_URL"],   # ← GitHub Pages landing page, NOT affiliate link
    "media_source": {
        "source_type": "media_id",
        "media_id": media_id
    }
}

GitHub Actions — All Three Pipelines in One File

yaml
# .github/workflows/affiliate_engine.yml

name: Affiliate Engine

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 0 * * *'      # Pipeline 1+2: daily midnight IST offset (UTC 18:30)
    - cron: '30 2,6,10,14 * * *'  # Pipeline 3: 4x/day pins
  workflow_dispatch:          # manual trigger for testing

jobs:

  discover-and-build:
    if: github.event.schedule == '0 0 * * *' || github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: '3.12' }
      - run: pip install requests beautifulsoup4 lxml gspread google-auth pillow
      - name: Discover Products
        run: python discover_products.py
        env:
          SHEET_ID: ${{ secrets.SHEET_ID }}
          AMAZON_ASSOCIATE_TAG: ${{ secrets.AMAZON_ASSOCIATE_TAG }}
          GEMINI_KEY: ${{ secrets.GEMINI_KEY }}
          GCP_CREDS_JSON: ${{ secrets.GCP_CREDS_JSON }}
      - name: Build Landing Pages
        run: python build_landing_page.py
        env:
          SHEET_ID: ${{ secrets.SHEET_ID }}
          GEMINI_KEY: ${{ secrets.GEMINI_KEY }}
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PAT_TOKEN }}
          GITHUB_REPO: ${{ github.repository }}
          AMAZON_ASSOCIATE_TAG: ${{ secrets.AMAZON_ASSOCIATE_TAG }}
          GCP_CREDS_JSON: ${{ secrets.GCP_CREDS_JSON }}

  post-pins:
    if: github.event.schedule == '30 2,6,10,14 * * *' || github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: '3.12' }
      - run: |
          pip install requests beautifulsoup4 lxml pillow gspread google-auth playwright
          playwright install chromium --with-deps
      - name: Post Pins (batch of 3-4 per run)
        run: python post_pin.py
        env:
          SHEET_ID: ${{ secrets.SHEET_ID }}
          GEMINI_KEY: ${{ secrets.GEMINI_KEY }}
          PINTEREST_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PINTEREST_TOKEN }}
          PINTEREST_BOARD_ID: ${{ secrets.PINTEREST_BOARD_ID }}
          GCP_CREDS_JSON: ${{ secrets.GCP_CREDS_JSON }}

Secrets to Add in GitHub

Go to your repo → Settings → Secrets and Variables → Actions → New repository secret:

Secret NameWhat It Is
SHEET_IDGoogle Sheet ID from URL
AMAZON_ASSOCIATE_TAGYour Associates tag e.g. yourtag-21
GEMINI_KEYGemini API key from aistudio.google.com
GCP_CREDS_JSONFull service account JSON (paste entire file contents)
PINTEREST_TOKENPinterest OAuth access token
PINTEREST_BOARD_IDYour Pinterest board ID
PAT_TOKENGitHub Personal Access Token with repo scope (for pushing pages)

Final Architecture Summary

Google Sheet (master DB)
    ↑ writes              ↓ reads
discover_products.py   post_pin.py
(daily)                (4x/day)
    ↓ writes              ↓ reads Page_URL
build_landing_page.py
(daily, after discovery)
    ↓ pushes HTML
GitHub Pages (free, indexed, SEO-able)
    ↑ Pinterest pins link here
    ↓ user clicks → Amazon affiliate link
        → your commission fires

Total cost: ₹0. Every component is free tier. The only thing this costs is the one-time setup time of about 3–4 hours.

You said: so tell me all the things i need to set up this whole pooject

so tell me all the things i need to set up this whole pooject

5 Apr

Claude responded: Let me give you a complete, no-fluff checklist of everything you need.

Let me give you a complete, no-fluff checklist of everything you need.

Phase 1 — Accounts to Create (Do These First)

1. Google Account (you likely have this)

  • Needs to be a dedicated account for this project, not your personal one
  • Everything Google-related runs under this one account

2. Amazon Associates India

  • Go to affiliate-program.amazon.in
  • Sign up with your Google account
  • You need a working website or social profile to get approved — use your GitHub Pages URL once it's set up (come back to this)
  • Note your Associate Tag (format: yourname-21)
  • Critical: you must make 3 qualifying sales within 180 days or the account gets deactivated

3. Pinterest Business Account

  • Go to pinterest.com/business/create
  • Convert to Business (free)
  • Go to developers.pinterest.com → Create App
  • Request scopes: pins:read, pins:write, boards:read, boards:write
  • Submit app for review (takes 1–3 days — do this first, it's your longest wait)
  • Create at least one board for your niche (e.g. "Hostel Engineer Picks")

4. Google Cloud Platform

  • Go to console.cloud.google.com
  • Create a new project (name it something like affiliate-engine)
  • Enable these APIs:
    • Google Sheets API
    • Google Drive API
  • Create a Service Account:
    • IAM & Admin → Service Accounts → Create
    • Role: Editor
    • Download the JSON key file — this becomes your GCP_CREDS_JSON secret
  • Note the service account email (looks like name@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com)

5. Google AI Studio (Gemini)

  • Go to aistudio.google.com
  • Get API Key → Create API Key
  • Free tier: 1,500 requests/day on Gemini 1.5 Flash — sufficient for your volume

6. GitHub Account

  • Go to github.com and create an account if you don't have one
  • Create a new public repository named hostel-engineer-picks (public = unlimited Actions minutes)
  • Enable GitHub Pages: repo Settings → Pages → Source: main branch → /root folder
